bins.name: Respect State and Retail Jurisdiction
bins.desc: The filer warns that large-load interconnections invariably touch state-jurisdictional retail service and local distribution facilities. Any Commission rule that expands scope or adopts a low threshold risks conflicts with state authority and resulting litigation.
quotes: the goals of the ANOPR will best be served by the Commission developing an appropriately targeted rule that does not disturb the significant work already underway in many states and regions to energize large loads, including data centers.

Issues regarding the appropriate scope of regional versus local planning and competition have been comprehensively addressed and decided by the Commission in those prior orders.  There is no reason to re-open those issues now, and certainly not in this proceeding, where meeting the goals of the ANOPR will require the Commission to move quickly and in a targeted manner.

bins.name: 200 MW Data-Center-Only Definition
bins.desc: The filer argues the 20 MW ANOPR threshold is too low and should be raised substantially, to at least 200 MW, and limited to data center customers. A broader definition sweeps in industrial and commercial customers with different needs and would make interconnection less efficient for everyone.
quotes: it is in the best interest of the Commission and all stakeholders to adopt a reasonably narrow definition that confines the application of the rules to the types of customers driving the ANOPR:  large data center loads.

the Commission should focus this rulemaking on data center customers that are driving the largest load additions and contributing to network upgrades, and not adopt a threshold so low that it undermines efficiency and adversely impacts traditional industrial and commercial load customers.

Applying generic rules to a broad swathe of large load customers will likely end up making load interconnections more inefficient, both for data center customers as well as traditional industrial and commercial customers that often have very different technical, financial, and regulatory needs.

bins.name: Reject Direct Assignment of Network Costs
bins.desc: The filer argues direct assignment of network upgrade costs contradicts decades of Commission precedent, discourages data center investment, and ignores the system-wide benefits of integrated upgrades. It supports retaining rolled-in rate treatment as the default.
quotes: A number of commenters indicate support for direct assignment of network transmission upgrade costs to large load customers, claiming that doing so best comports with cost causation principles.  Their assertions are wrong and contrary to decades of Commission precedent governing transmission pricing policy.

transmission upgrades, even when prompted in whole or part by data center interconnections can, and often do, involve significant work on integrated facilities that serve customers across wide geographic areas.

Direct assignment of network upgrade costs dramatically increases the costs to the data center and will likely reduce the number of data centers that interconnect and slow others that are sufficiently brave to weather the process.

the better approach, and one that avoids protracted cost allocation disputes, as well as better respects jurisdictional boundaries, is to continue to apply the Commission's longstanding preference for rolled-in rate treatment as the default cost allocation methodology for large load driven network upgrades.

bins.name: Full Study of Hybrid Load and Generation
bins.desc: The filer opposes net-basis study for hybrid facilities, stating that studying load and generation only at net amounts masks real reliability needs and can shift costs to other customers when on-site generation goes offline.
quotes: Load is not competing with generation for access to the system and is therefore not similarly situated to generation.
on-site load and generation need to be studied at their full amounts to ensure reliable interconnection.

bins.name: Rolled-In Rates as Default Policy
bins.desc: The filer strongly supports preserving rolled-in rate treatment and encourages service agreements with consumer protection terms as the vehicle for managing cost shifts, rather than direct assignment or holding wholesale entities responsible for a single customer's upgrade costs.
quotes: A number of commenters indicate support for direct assignment of network transmission upgrade costs to large load customers, claiming that doing so best comports with cost causation principles.  Their assertions are wrong and contrary to decades of Commission precedent governing transmission pricing policy.

transmission upgrades, even when prompted in whole or part by data center interconnections can, and often do, involve significant work on integrated facilities that serve customers across wide geographic areas.

Direct assignment of network upgrade costs dramatically increases the costs to the data center and will likely reduce the number of data centers that interconnect and slow others that are sufficiently brave to weather the process.

the better approach, and one that avoids protracted cost allocation disputes, as well as better respects jurisdictional boundaries, is to continue to apply the Commission's longstanding preference for rolled-in rate treatment as the default cost allocation methodology for large load driven network upgrades.
the Commission should instead consider encouraging transmission providers to develop and submit for filing large load service agreements that contain comprehensive customer protection mechanisms

bins.name: Full-Amount Study for Hybrid Facilities
bins.desc: The filer supports studying load and generation from hybrid facilities at full amounts, not on a net basis, to correctly size required upgrades and prevent reliability gaps and cost shifts.
quotes: on-site load and generation need to be studied at their full amounts to ensure reliable interconnection.

bins.name: Keep ANOPR Narrowly Targeted
bins.desc: The filer argues the Commission should decline to address issues outside the ANOPR's core purpose, including regional planning competition, long-term planning under Order 1920, load forecasting requirements, and mandates for specific technologies. Expanding scope will slow the rulemaking and increase litigation.
quotes: the goals of the ANOPR will best be served by the Commission developing an appropriately targeted rule that does not disturb the significant work already underway in many states and regions to energize large loads, including data centers.
Failure to do so will result in a larger, more confused record, a risk of increased litigation, and create more hurdles for both transmission providers and data center customers.

Issues regarding the appropriate scope of regional versus local planning and competition have been comprehensively addressed and decided by the Commission in those prior orders.  There is no reason to re-open those issues now, and certainly not in this proceeding, where meeting the goals of the ANOPR will require the Commission to move quickly and in a targeted manner.

bins.name: Distinguish Data Centers from Other Large Loads
bins.desc: The filer argues that data center customers have distinct speed-to-power needs and financial profiles that differ from traditional industrial and commercial customers, and generic rules applied across all large loads will harm customers the ANOPR was not meant to cover.
quotes: Applying generic rules to a broad swathe of large load customers will likely end up making load interconnections more inefficient, both for data center customers as well as traditional industrial and commercial customers that often have very different technical, financial, and regulatory needs.

bins.name: Service Agreements Over Mandatory Rules
bins.desc: Rather than a direct-assignment mandate, the filer recommends the Commission encourage transmission providers to file large-load service agreements that include minimum charges, early termination fees, and other consumer protections to manage cost shifts for both retail and wholesale customers.
quotes: the Commission should instead consider encouraging transmission providers to develop and submit for filing large load service agreements that contain comprehensive customer protection mechanisms

bins.name: Oppose Load Customer Option to Build
bins.desc: The filer opposes extending the option to build to large load customers. Generator interconnection experience shows the supervision burden eliminates projected savings, and load is not comparable to generation because it is not competing for access to the system.
quotes: experience with generator interconnections, as well as a recognition of the differences between generators and load customers, demonstrates otherwise.
When factoring in these additional requirements, any potential efficiencies or cost savings are rendered de minimis or non-existent.
Load is not competing with generation for access to the system and is therefore not similarly situated to generation.
